perturbation.original_text: The term “ affiliate ” has the meaning set forth in Rule 12b-2 of the regulations promulgated under Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the “ Exchange Act ”).
perturbation.changed_text: The term “ affiliate ” shall mean any entity controlled by, controlling, or under common control with a party, irrespective of the definition in Rule 12b-2 of the regulations promulgated under Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the “ Exchange Act ”).
perturbation.explanation: By defining 'affiliate' differently from the Exchange Act, the contract creates ambiguity and potential non-compliance issues, especially considering the standstill provision relying on Exchange Act definitions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 12b-2 provides a specific definition of 'control' for determining affiliate status under the Exchange Act. The modified text disregards this definition, potentially leading to inconsistencies in applying securities laws, especially related to beneficial ownership and reporting requirements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (3) Entering and exiting accelerated filer and large accelerated filer status. (i) Th
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'affiliate' irrespective of the definition in Rule 12b-2 of the Exchange Act. This contradicts the original contract, which specifically adopts the Rule 12b-2 definition. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which defines various terms under 17 CFR § 240.12b-2, including 'accelerated filer' and 'large accelerated filer', emphasizes the importance of adhering to the definitions within this regulation unless the context requires otherwise. By explicitly disregarding this definition, the changed text introduces a conflicting standard for determining affiliate status, which could lead to inconsistencies in applying securities laws.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of being furnished the Proprietary Information by the other party, each party agrees that, for a period of twelve (12) months from the date of this Agreement, such party and its Restricted Affiliates shall not employ or solicit to employ any of the current officers or employees of the other party or its subsidiaries with whom such party or its Representatives has had contact in connection with the evaluation of the Possible Transaction or who were specifically identified to such party during the period of investigation of the other party pursuant to this Agreement, without obtaining the prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of being furnished the Proprietary Information by the other party, each party agrees that, for a period of twelve (12) months from the date of this Agreement, such party and its Restricted Affiliates shall not 'encourage' any of the current officers or employees of the other party or its subsidiaries to consider new employment opportunities.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'solicit to employ' has a specific meaning under labor law and case law that involves active recruitment. Replacing it with 'encourage to consider' broadens the restriction, making it potentially unenforceable and in conflict with employee's rights to seek employment.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 8(a)(1) of the NLRA protects employees' rights to organize and bargain collectively. While non-solicitation agreements are generally permissible, overly broad restrictions on encouraging employees to consider other opportunities may be deemed an unfair labor practice, especially if they interfere with employees' rights to discuss employment terms and conditions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: U.S.C. Title 29 - LABOR 29 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 29 - LABOR CHAPTER 7 - LABOR-MANAGEMENT RELATIONS SUBCHAPTER II - NATIONAL LABOR RELATIONS Sec. 158 - Unfair labor practices From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §158. Unfair labor practices (a) Unfair labor practices by employer It shall be an unfair labor practice for an employer— (1) to interfere with, restrain, or coerce employees in the exercise of the rights guaranteed in section 157 of this title; (2) to dominate or interfere with the formation or administration of any labor organization or contribute financial or other support to it: Provided , That subject to rules and regulations made and published by the Board pursuant to section 156 of this title, an employer shall not be prohibited from permitting employees to confer with him during working hours without loss of time or pay; (3) by discrimination in regard to hire or tenure of employment or any term or condition of employment to encourage or discourage membership in any labor organization: Provided , That nothing in this subchapter, or in any other statute of the United States, shall preclude an employer from making an agreement with a labor organization (not established, maintained, or assisted by any action defined in this subsection as an unfair labor practice) to require as a condition of employment membership therein on or after the thirtieth day following the beginning of such employment or the effective date of such agreement, whichever is the later, (i) if such labor organization is the representative of the employees as provided in section 159(a) of this title, in the appropriate collective-bargaining unit covered by such agreement when made, and (ii) unless following an election held as provided in section 159(e) of this title within one year preceding the effective date of such agreement, the Board shall have certified that at least a majority of the employees eligible to vote in such election have voted to rescind the authority of such labor organization to make such an agreement: Provided further , That no employer shall justify any discrimination against an employee for nonmembership in a labor organization (A) if he has reasonable grounds for believing that such membership was not available to the employee on the same terms and conditions generally applicable to other
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'solicit to employ' with 'encourage to consider,' broadening the restriction on employee movement. This broader restriction, as explained, could interfere with employees' rights to discuss employment terms and conditions, potentially violating Section 8(a)(1) of the NLRA, which prohibits employers from interfering with employees' rights. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 29 U.S.C. §158(a)(1), explicitly states that it's an unfair labor practice for an employer to 'interfere with, restrain, or coerce employees in the exercise of the rights guaranteed in section 157.' The original text, using 'solicit to employ,' is more narrowly defined, while the changed text's 'encourage to consider' could be interpreted as such interference, leading to a contradiction with the NLRA.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of New York and will be binding upon and inure to the benefit of the parties hereto and their respective successors and assigns.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of Switzerland, irrespective of conflicts of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: Choosing Swiss law and disregarding 'conflicts of law' principles might contradict established legal precedents regarding contract enforcement, particularly if the contract's performance predominantly occurs in another jurisdiction (e.g., New York or Canada).
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Principles
perturbation.law_citation: New York Choice of Law Principles
perturbation.law_explanation: New York courts typically apply a 'grouping of contacts' or 'center of gravity' test to determine which jurisdiction's law governs a contract, even if the contract specifies a particular jurisdiction. Disregarding conflicts of law principles could lead to the chosen law being deemed unenforceable if the contract's connections to the chosen jurisdiction are minimal.
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies Swiss law and disregards conflicts of law principles. The Law Explanation states that New York courts apply a 'grouping of contacts' or 'center of gravity' test. By disregarding conflicts of law, the contract risks enforceability if connections to Switzerland are minimal, potentially contradicting established legal precedents. While the snippets are missing, the Law Explanation supports the determination of a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party further covenants and agrees, in consideration of it being furnished with Proprietary Information, that neither it nor any of its affiliates who have received or had access to the Proprietary Information of the other party (a “ Restricted Affiliate ”) will, at any time commencing on the date hereof and ending on the earliest of (a) the twelve (12) month anniversary of this Agreement, (b) the execution and delivery by Fairfax (or its affiliate(s)) and Allied World of a definitive agreement to effect the Possible Transaction and (c) a Fundamental Change Event (as defined below) with respect to the other party, directly or indirectly, unless requested in writing (or by email) by the other party’s chief executive officer, chairman or board of directors: (i) acquire (or agree, offer, seek or propose to acquire, in each case, publicly or privately), by purchase, tender offer, exchange offer, agreement or business amalgamation or combination or in any other manner, any ownership, including beneficial ownership, as defined in Rule 13d-3 under the Exchange Act, of any material assets or businesses or any securities of the other party or any of its subsidiaries, or any rights or options to acquire such ownership (including from any third party);
perturbation.changed_text: Each party further covenants and agrees, in consideration of it being furnished with Proprietary Information, that neither it nor any of its affiliates who have received or had access to the Proprietary Information of the other party (a “ Restricted Affiliate ”) will, at any time commencing on the date hereof, directly or indirectly acquire any ownership of the other party or any of its subsidiaries, *unless such acquisition is passive*.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'unless such acquisition is passive' to the standstill provision creates a loophole. 'Passive investment' is a term of art under securities laws with specific requirements (e.g., intent, voting rights). Without defining it in the contract, it invites disputes over what constitutes a passive investment and could contradict the intent of the standstill.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13(d) requires disclosure of beneficial ownership exceeding 5%. The modified text introduces ambiguity as to what constitutes a passive investment exempt from the standstill, potentially allowing actions that would otherwise trigger disclosure requirements or violate the spirit of the standstill agreement. The undefined term undermines the intended protective measures against hostile takeovers or undue influence.
perturbation.justification: The addition of 'unless such acquisition is passive' introduces ambiguity that weakens the standstill provision. The Law Explanation notes the ambiguity undermines the protective measures against hostile takeovers. The original text restricts any acquisition, but the changed text introduces a loophole allowing for 'passive' acquisitions. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 only lists sections of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, the Law Explanation discusses Section 13(d) and its implications for disclosure. The changed text undermines the original intent, creating a legal contradiction because the term 'passive' lacks a clear contractual definition, potentially conflicting with legal standards for beneficial ownership and disclosure requirements.

perturbation.original_text: It is understood and agreed that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement and that each party shall be entitled to specific performance as a remedy for any such breach or threatened breach of this Agreement. This remedy shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedy for any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.changed_text: It is understood and agreed that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement, *except for breaches relating to the non-solicitation clause.*
perturbation.explanation: Carving out the non-solicitation clause from the specific performance remedy implies that only monetary damages are available for its breach. However, courts often grant injunctive relief (a form of specific performance) in non-solicitation cases to prevent further harm.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Law on Non-Solicitation Agreements
perturbation.law_citation: New York Law on Non-Solicitation Agreements
perturbation.law_explanation: New York courts routinely grant injunctive relief (a form of specific performance) to enforce non-solicitation agreements, especially when trade secrets or confidential information are at risk. The modified text restricts the available remedies, potentially preventing a party from obtaining necessary injunctive relief to protect its interests.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The modified text limits the remedy for breaches of the non-solicitation clause to monetary damages, removing the possibility of specific performance (injunctive relief). The Law Explanation indicates that New York courts often grant injunctive relief in non-solicitation cases to protect interests. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines specific performance as a contractual remedy where a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations when monetary damages are inadequate. By carving out the non-solicitation clause from specific performance, the modified contract contradicts established legal practices and limits the remedies available to a degree that it undermines the legal protection typically afforded in these cases.
